title: Bhindi Masala

overview.description: A North Indian dish of okra cooked with onion, tomato and spices until tender while retaining its shape and texture.

method: Wash the okra and dry it extremely thoroughly.
Cut the okra into pieces approximately 2–3cm long.
Heat 1½ tbsp of the oil in a large frying pan.
Add the okra and cook over medium-high heat for 8–10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it is tender and has developed some colour.
Remove the okra and set aside.
Add the remaining oil to the pan.
Add the sliced onion and cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the ginger, garlic and green chilli if using.
Add the tomatoes, ground coriander, ground cumin, turmeric, chilli powder, amchur and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes soften and the masala thickens.
Return the okra to the pan.
Stir gently to coat it in the masala.
Cook for a further 5–8 minutes until the okra is fully tender.
Finish with fresh coriander and serve.

learning.did-you-know: Okra is known as bhindi in Hindi and is used widely across Indian cuisines. Its characteristic texture comes from a naturally occurring substance that becomes more noticeable when the vegetable is wet or cut.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to prepare and cook okra while controlling its natural slimy texture and how dry cooking can help preserve its shape.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A useful introduction to okra that teaches the children how preparation and cooking technique can dramatically affect the texture of a vegetable.
chapman-tips: Dry the okra extremely well before cutting it; moisture makes the texture much more slippery.
Don't overcrowd the pan when cooking the okra.
Cooking the okra separately before adding it to the masala helps control its texture.
Avoid adding water unless absolutely necessary.
Amchur provides a characteristic sourness, but lemon juice can be used if you do not have it.
Don't stir too aggressively or the okra may break apart.
If the children are unsure about okra, serve it alongside familiar chapati or rice rather than hiding it in another dish.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The texture becomes softer after refrigeration and freezing, although leftovers can still be reheated and eaten.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with chapati or use as a filling for a flatbread wrap.
